retrieved_docs.title: Zika Virus -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Diseases caused by Zika virus are predominately arboviral and transmitted by the bite of female Aedes aegypti and Aedes albopictus mosquitoes. Person-to-person contact (e.g., sexual contact), blood transfusion, organ transplantation, and perinatally (maternal-fetal vertical transmission) may also transmit infection. Zika virus is related to multiple other arboviral causes of human diseases, including Japanese encephalitis virus, tick-borne encephalitis virus, West Nile virus, dengue virus, and the yellow fever virus. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Zika Virus -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Zika virus infection should be considered if the patient has a history of recent travel to an area with suspected Zika virus transmission or a sexual partner with recent travel to such an area. The duration of infectivity of bodily fluids following infection is unknown. However, Zika virus has been cultured from semen up to 90 days after symptom onset, and viral RNA has been detected in blood after 58 days and in semen up to 188 days.

retrieved_docs.title: Zika Virus -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy and Zika virus infection

retrieved_docs.title: Transfusion Transmitted Disease -- Etiology -- Viral Organisms
retrieved_docs.content: Zika virus: A flavivirus; transmission of Zika virus occurs via the bite of an infected mosquito, maternal-fetal transmission, sexual activity, transfusion of blood products, and organ transplantation.

retrieved_docs.title: Zika Virus -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Zika virus was first identified in Uganda in 1947 during a study of yellow fever virus. The first report of human infection was in 1954 in Nigeria. Subsequent studies suggested widespread distribution in Africa and Asia. Until 2014, there was no evidence of Zika virus in the Americas. Before 2007, there were no reports of large outbreaks of Zika infections. In 2007, an outbreak was identified on the coast of Central Africa, following suspected dengue and chikungunya epidemics. That same year, an outbreak was also identified in Micronesia (western Pacific Ocean). French Polynesia experienced outbreaks in 2013 and 2014 with subsequent outbreaks in 2015 to 2016, on other Pacific islands, including New Caledonia, Easter Island, Cook Islands, Samoa, and American Samoa. Cases of Zika infection were reported in Brazil in late 2014 and early 2015. It then rapidly spread through South and Central America. The first reported case of locally transmitted Zika in the continental United States was the week of July 24, 2016. As of April 19, 2017, 223 cases of Zika from presumed local, mosquito-borne transmission, have been identified (primarily in Florida) and 76 cases transmitted via other routes (46 sexual, 28 congenital, and two other) have been reported in the United States. [5] [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Zika Virus -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Zika virus is a single-stranded RNA virus of the family Flavivirus and the genus Flavivirus and belongs to two phylogenetic types: Asian and African.  In the majority of people, infection by the Zika virus is mild and self-limiting. [1] [2] [3] In most cases, Zika infection is a mild self-limited illness. Today, zika virus infection is a reportable illness.

retrieved_docs.title: Transfusion Transmitted Disease -- Epidemiology -- Viral Infection
retrieved_docs.content: Zika virus: Approximately 20% to 25% of patients infected with Zika virus develop symptomatic infection. Although no confirmed cases of Zika virus transmission via blood transfusion have been reported, there have been confirmed cases of transmission via platelet transfusion. While 1 study suggests that women and patients younger than 40 are more likely to develop symptomatic infections, age and sex do not appear to influence overall infection rates.

retrieved_docs.title: Zika Virus -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The Zika virus is transmitted by the Aedes mosquito and several other Aedes species. Besides a mosquito bite, the virus can also be transmitted sexually.

retrieved_docs.title: Antepartum Infections -- Epidemiology -- Zika virus
retrieved_docs.content: The mosquitoes in the Aedes genus spread the Zika virus, and an unknown primate reservoir is theorized to exist. [22] The virus has been found in the semen and vaginal secretions of infected individuals and sexual transmission of the virus has been documented. [23] Nonsexual human-to-human transmission has also been reported, although the mechanism of infection in these cases has not been elucidated. [24] [25] Vertical transmission occurs in utero through infection or injury of the placenta, resulting in the transplacental transmission of the virus to the fetal brain. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Antepartum Infections -- Epidemiology -- Zika virus
retrieved_docs.content: Zika virus is a significant infectious disease due to its impact on fetal neurodevelopment. Initially identified in tropical and subtropical regions, such as certain areas of South America, Zika has since spread to various parts of the world where Aedes mosquitoes are prevalent, as these mosquitoes serve as the primary vectors for the transmission of the disease. The peak incidence of Zika virus infections occurred in 2016. Prenatal exposure to the virus can lead to a spectrum of complications, ranging from severe microcephaly to more subtle neurodevelopmental delays that may not become apparent until later in childhood, even in the absence of obvious defects at birth. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Vertical Transplacental Infections -- Etiology -- Vector-Borne Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Zika virus (ZIKV) is a vector-borne disease with maternal-fetal relevance. ZIKV is transmitted primarily through bites from infected Aedes mosquitoes and through sexual contact with infected individuals, blood transfusions, and contact with bodily fluids. Once a pregnant individual is infected, ZIKV is suspected to infect trophoblasts, cross the placental and blood-brain barriers, and infect brain endothelial cells. [38] In addition, ZIKV appears to disrupt the permeability of tight junctions, permitting paracellular transplacental but not blood-brain barrier transmission. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Zika Virus -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Prevention is the key intervention to avoid infection. This prevention includes using insect repellants, wearing appropriate clothing (long-sleeved shirts and pants), eliminating mosquito breeding sites (standing water in tires, birdbaths), and preventing entry of mosquitos into homes (screens). After traveling to a known Zika virus area, travelers should avoid mosquito bites for several weeks to prevent introduction locally.

retrieved_docs.title: Zika Virus -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Currently, there is no treatment for Zika virus complicating pregnancy.  Prevention is key. Pregnant women should avoid mosquito exposure, and avoid travel to known Zika virus areas, if possible. If unable to avoid exposure, testing, as described above, is recommended. If planning to become pregnant, the woman should avoid conception (abstinence or use of condoms) for at least 8 weeks after exposure or the start of symptoms. Men should avoid the risk of their partner conceiving (abstinence or use of condoms) for at least six months after exposure or initial symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: Arbovirus Encephalitides -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Zika virus, another mosquito-borne flavivirus, is primarily transmitted via Aedes aegypti , although it can also be transmitted by Aedes albopictus . Transmission can also occur via maternal-fetal transmission, sexual transmission, blood products, organ transplantation, and laboratory exposures. [22] This virus can cause multiple neurological complications, including encephalitis. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Zika Virus -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: During the current outbreak, the Brazilian Ministry of Health noted an unusual rise of newborns with microcephaly and other neurologic disorders and malformations. In the United States, fetuses and infants with congenital disabilities and confirmed Zika infections appear to be 30 times higher than baseline and infection during the first trimester had a higher proportion of deficits. Among completed pregnancies, laboratory evidence of Zika infection was associated with an incidence of birth defects of 5%. Congenital disabilities currently identified as associated with infection include: Microcephaly with partially collapsed skull Decreased brain tissue with brain damage Damage to the posterior eye Limited range of joint movement Increased muscle tone restricting body movement after birth The complete spectrum of disorders associated with congenital Zika virus infection is not yet known. [14] [15] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Transfusion Transmitted Disease -- Etiology -- Viral Organisms
retrieved_docs.content: West Nile virus: An enveloped ssRNA virus within the family Flaviviridae, WNV threatens many transfusion services worldwide. [18] Transmission among humans exists through mosquito bites, blood transfusion, intrauterine exposure, breastfeeding, and organ transplantation. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2263
retrieved_docs.content: Navigational concept is classified as an arthropod‑borne viral encephalitis (التهاب الدماغ الفيروسي المنقول بالمفصليات) and is also linked to immune system disorders (اضطراب الجهاز المناعي), indicating that this condition both belongs to the group of viral encephalitides transmitted by arthropods and falls within the broader category of immune dysregulation. This overlap highlights the interplay between infectious encephalitic processes and immune system pathology, which is important for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 359
retrieved_docs.content: Remdesivir is an antiviral medication that may be used to treat a viral infection (عدوى فيروسية). This therapeutic relationship highlights Remdesivir’s potential role in managing viral diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2766
retrieved_docs.content: The relationship “Allowed qualifier” connects enzymology with both gestational diabetes (داء السكري أثناء الحمل) and seizure (نوبة), indicating that enzymological considerations are permissible descriptors for these clinical conditions. In addition, the same “Allowed qualifier” links arboviry with enzymology, suggesting that enzymological analysis is an appropriate qualifier when studying arboviral contexts. This network of qualifiers underscores the relevance of enzymology across diverse medical areas, from metabolic disorders in pregnancy and neurological events to the investigation of arboviral infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 280
retrieved_docs.content: Direct acting antivirals are classified as a subset of antivirals for systemic use, indicating that these agents are administered systemically and act directly on viral targets. This relationship places direct acting antivirals within the broader category of systemic antiviral therapies, helping clinicians recognize that they belong to the larger group of antivirals intended for systemic application.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 627
retrieved_docs.content: Remdesivir may be used to treat عدوى فيروسية, offering therapeutic benefit for patients with viral infections, whereas ribavirin may be employed to prevent عدوى فيروسية, serving as a prophylactic antiviral option. Both agents are relevant in the clinical management of viral diseases, with remdesivir focusing on treatment and ribavirin on prevention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1590
retrieved_docs.content: Other infections, including parasitic infections, are classified as a subcategory under the broader parent disease group داء الريكتسيات.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1623
retrieved_docs.content: Zanamivir is classified as a member of the neuraminidase inhibitors, which are direct‑acting antivirals. As a neuraminidase inhibitor, zanamivir targets the viral neuraminidase enzyme, thereby blocking the release of progeny influenza viruses and inhibiting viral replication. This relationship places zanamivir within the broader group of direct‑acting antiviral agents that act specifically on viral proteins to treat infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 0 -- Community 3
retrieved_docs.content: preventive and therapeutic use enables precise prescribing, optimized patient care, and effective public‑health interventions across viral diseases such as HIV, ocular viral infections, influenza, smallpox, and related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 285
retrieved_docs.content: Ribavirin is classified as a member of the group of antivirals used for the treatment of hepatitis C virus (HCV) infections, indicating its role within this therapeutic class. In addition to its use against HCV, ribavirin may also help prevent viral infections (عدوى فيروسية). Remdesivir, another antiviral agent, may be employed to treat viral infections (عدوى فيروسية). Together, ribavirin and remdesivir exemplify antiviral medications that are utilized both for treating specific viral diseases such as HCV and for broader management of viral infections, underscoring their importance in antiviral therapy and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1229
retrieved_docs.content: Tecovirimat may be used to treat ال​جدري (smallpox), and ال​جدري is classified as a viral infection. This relationship highlights that tecovirimat’s therapeutic potential is directed toward a disease that belongs to the broader category of viral infections.

retrieved_docs.content: planning, diagnostic work‑up, and parasitological evaluation to a wide spectrum of congenital, metabolic, infectious, endocrine, renal, ophthalmic, reproductive, and neurological conditions, thereby supporting comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 62
retrieved_docs.content: the importance of recognizing contraindicated drug classes, and the role of antiviral agents such as zanamivir in preventing viral respiratory illnesses that can lead to secondary bacterial or fungal pneumonias.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 655
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections include ribavirin and telaprevir, both of which are employed as therapeutic agents against hepatitis C virus. Remdesivir has been identified as a medication that may treat عدوى فيروسية (viral infection), while ribavirin is also noted for its potential to prevent عدوى فيروسية. Together, these antiviral drugs illustrate the spectrum of strategies used to both treat and prevent viral diseases, underscoring their importance in clinical management of infections such as hepatitis C and other viral pathogens.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2810
retrieved_docs.content: Protease inhibitors, direct acting antivirals constitute a class of antiviral medications that includes ritonavir, atazanavir, and tipranavir. These agents function by inhibiting viral protease enzymes, thereby preventing the maturation of viral particles and serving as direct-acting antiviral therapies, particularly in the management of HIV infection. Their classification together reflects shared mechanisms of action and therapeutic use within antiviral treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 79
retrieved_docs.content: Other CNS infection and poliomyelitis is classified as a subcategory of التهاب سنجابية البصلة (شلل الأطفال البصلي), indicating that this group of central nervous system infections, including poliomyelitis, falls under the broader diagnosis of bulbar poliomyelitis. This relationship highlights that bulbar poliomyelitis encompasses various CNS infections that affect the bulbar region, and understanding this hierarchy aids clinicians in recognizing the spectrum of neurological involvement associated with poliovirus and related infections.
